mnemonics.title: Strong vs Weak Connectivity
mnemonics.content: STRONG needs Paths Both Ways (←→), WEAK just needs Undirected connection (no arrows). Remember: STRONG = BOTH directions required.

mnemonics.title: Eulerian Digraph Conditions
mnemonics.content: EULER = EUal (equal) + Strong (strongly connected). The word 'Euler' itself has 'equal' hidden in it!

mnemonics.title: Tournament Properties
mnemonics.content: TOURNAMENT has ONE winner per match - exactly ONE directed edge between each pair.

mnemonics.title: DAG Meaning
mnemonics.content: DAG = Directed Acyclic Graph. Remember: DAGs have No Cycles - like a one-way street with no loops!

acronyms.usage: Used in project scheduling, task dependencies, topological sorting
Maximal subgraph where every pair of vertices is mutually reachable

acronyms.expansion: Eulerian - Equal (indeg = outdeg) + Strong connectivity + Circuit (closed path)
acronyms.usage: Determines if a directed trail using all edges exactly once exists

acronyms.expansion: TC adds edges for all reachable pairs (T→C means T-racing to C-over all paths)
acronyms.usage: Finding all reachable vertices in a digraph

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a directed graph as a city with one-way streets. Strongly connected means you can drive from any intersection to any other following street directions.
Same city, but if you ignore street directions (imagine bidirectional), can you reach everywhere? That's weak connectivity.
Think of a sports tournament bracket where every team plays every other team exactly once - one winner per match, one arrow per pair.
Family tree ancestry going only backward in time - you can go from parent to child but never back to parent (no cycles).
Grid where rows = starting points, columns = destinations. 1 means 'can go from here to there', 0 means 'cannot'.

analogies.analogy: Think of edges like one-way streets - they have a specific direction you MUST follow, unlike undirected edges which are two-way roads.
Like a round-trip flight network where you can fly from any city to any other city (possibly with layovers) in both directions.
A tennis knockout bracket where every player plays every other player once - exactly one winner between any two players.
Building a house: you must complete foundation before walls, walls before roof - tasks have dependencies that form a DAG.
Indegree is like number of people who report TO you (inputs), outdegree is like number of people you supervise (outputs).
